req.title: Account-level transaction limits (boundary variant)
req.statement: A customer shall configure daily per-channel limits within regulatory caps; changes take effect after a 2-hour cool-down. Boundary behaviour at exact min/max thresholds must be tested.

doc.reasoning: R-FIN-042 governs three testable axes: (1) valid limit submission within regulatory caps, (2) rejection when a submitted value exceeds the regulatory cap, and (3) exact boundary behaviour at the min/max threshold values. A cool-down state transition is also testable. Five tests are designed: one EP positive (valid mid-range limit), one NEG negative (over-cap rejection), one BVA boundary (exact max threshold), one BVA boundary (exact min threshold = 0 or regulatory floor), and one STC state-transition (pending → effective after cool-down). Each test is self-contained via fixtures that create and tear down a test account, uses deterministic amounts derived from documented regulatory constants, and makes a single focused assertion per behaviour.

tests.name: test_submit_valid_limit_within_regulatory_cap
preconditions: Customer is authenticated with a valid bearer token
Account identified by ACCOUNT_ID exists and is active
Submitted daily limit (5000) is strictly within the regulatory cap
expected: Response body contains status field equal to 'pending'
Response body contains an effective_at timestamp approximately 2 hours in the future
trace: R-FIN-042: customer configures daily per-channel limits within regulatory caps
R-FIN-042: changes take effect after a 2-hour cool-down
R-FIN-042: expected outcome — pending limit stored
tests.executable:
import pytest
import time
from datetime import datetime, timezone

def test_submit_valid_limit_within_regulatory_cap(auth_client, test_account):
    account_id = test_account["id"]
    payload = {"channel": "online", "daily_limit": 5000}

    resp = auth_client.patch(
        f"/accounts/{account_id}/limits",
        json=payload
    )

    assert resp.status_code == 202, f"Expected 202, got {resp.status_code}: {resp.text}"
    body = resp.json()
    assert body["status"] == "pending"
    effective_at = datetime.fromisoformat(body["effective_at"].replace("Z", "+00:00"))
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    delta_hours = (effective_at - now).total_seconds() / 3600
    assert 1.9 <= delta_hours <= 2.1, f"Cool-down window outside expected range: {delta_hours}h"

tests.name: test_submit_limit_exceeding_regulatory_cap_returns_422
preconditions: Customer is authenticated with a valid bearer token
Account identified by ACCOUNT_ID exists and is active
Submitted daily limit (REGULATORY_CAP + 1) exceeds the regulatory cap
expected: Response body contains an error code referencing the regulatory cap violation
trace: R-FIN-042: error pathway — 422 when over regulatory cap
tests.executable:
import pytest

REGULATORY_CAP = <REGULATORY_CAP_CONSTANT>

def test_submit_limit_exceeding_regulatory_cap_returns_422(auth_client, test_account):
    account_id = test_account["id"]
    over_cap_value = REGULATORY_CAP + 1
    payload = {"channel": "online", "daily_limit": over_cap_value}

    resp = auth_client.patch(
        f"/accounts/{account_id}/limits",
        json=payload
    )

    assert resp.status_code == 422, f"Expected 422, got {resp.status_code}: {resp.text}"
    body = resp.json()
    error_detail = str(body).lower()
    assert "cap" in error_detail or "limit" in error_detail or "regulatory" in error_detail, \
        f"Error body does not reference cap violation: {body}"

tests.name: test_submit_limit_at_exact_regulatory_cap_boundary
preconditions: Customer is authenticated with a valid bearer token
Account identified by ACCOUNT_ID exists and is active
Submitted daily limit equals exactly the regulatory cap (max valid boundary)
expected: HTTP 202 Accepted (exact max boundary is inclusive)
trace: R-FIN-042: boundary behaviour at exact max threshold must be tested
tests.executable:
import pytest

REGULATORY_CAP = <REGULATORY_CAP_CONSTANT>

def test_submit_limit_at_exact_regulatory_cap_boundary(auth_client, test_account):
    account_id = test_account["id"]
    payload = {"channel": "online", "daily_limit": REGULATORY_CAP}

    resp = auth_client.patch(
        f"/accounts/{account_id}/limits",
        json=payload
    )

    assert resp.status_code == 202, \
        f"Exact cap boundary should be accepted (202), got {resp.status_code}: {resp.text}"
    body = resp.json()
    assert body["status"] == "pending"

tests.name: test_submit_limit_at_minimum_boundary
preconditions: Customer is authenticated with a valid bearer token
Account identified by ACCOUNT_ID exists and is active
Submitted daily limit equals the regulatory minimum (0 or defined floor)
expected: HTTP 202 Accepted (minimum boundary is inclusive if 0 is a valid floor)
trace: R-FIN-042: boundary behaviour at exact min threshold must be tested
tests.executable:
import pytest

MIN_LIMIT = <REGULATORY_MIN_CONSTANT>

def test_submit_limit_at_minimum_boundary(auth_client, test_account):
    account_id = test_account["id"]
    payload = {"channel": "online", "daily_limit": MIN_LIMIT}

    resp = auth_client.patch(
        f"/accounts/{account_id}/limits",
        json=payload
    )

    assert resp.status_code == 202, \
        f"Minimum boundary ({MIN_LIMIT}) should be accepted, got {resp.status_code}: {resp.text}"
    body = resp.json()
    assert body["status"] == "pending"

tests.name: test_pending_limit_transitions_to_effective_after_cooldown
preconditions: Customer is authenticated with a valid bearer token
Account identified by ACCOUNT_ID exists and is active
A limit change has been submitted and is in 'pending' state
System clock can be advanced OR test uses a time-travel helper to simulate cool-down expiry
actions: PATCH /accounts/{id}/limits to create a pending limit
Invoke <advance_system_clock_fn> or <time_travel_helper> to move time forward by 2 hours + 1 minute
GET /accounts/{id}/limits to read current effective limit
expected: GET response contains the previously submitted limit value
GET response shows status field equal to 'effective'
No pending entry remains for the same channel
trace: R-FIN-042: changes take effect after a 2-hour cool-down
R-FIN-042: expected outcome — effective after cool-down
tests.executable:
import pytest

def test_pending_limit_transitions_to_effective_after_cooldown(
    auth_client, test_account, advance_clock
):
    account_id = test_account["id"]
    new_limit = 3000
    payload = {"channel": "online", "daily_limit": new_limit}

    patch_resp = auth_client.patch(
        f"/accounts/{account_id}/limits", json=payload
    )
    assert patch_resp.status_code == 202
    assert patch_resp.json()["status"] == "pending"

    advance_clock(hours=2, minutes=1)

    get_resp = auth_client.get(f"/accounts/{account_id}/limits")
    assert get_resp.status_code == 200
    limits = get_resp.json()["limits"]
    online_limit = next((l for l in limits if l["channel"] == "online"), None)
    assert online_limit is not None, "online channel limit not found"
    assert online_limit["daily_limit"] == new_limit
    assert online_limit["status"] == "effective"
